body_txt: The day-to-day life of an elite athlete is extremely physically demanding, between balancing training, competitions, management, and everything else that goes into keeping their body in tip-top shape. But aging comes for us all, and often these top athletes find their bodies less and less capable of keeping up . They tire faster, take longer to recover from illness and/or injury, and are slower to pick up new styles of play. What's more, there's often a new star on the rise threatening to replace them. Soon enough, the pundits are asking when they will retire instead of lauding their achievements. This naturally takes a mental toll. Often, these elite athletes love winning and/or being in the spotlight, and are reluctant to be phased out in favor of the stronger, better, faster new guard. They may be discomfited by the knowledge that they're one Career-Ending Injury away from forced retirement. If they feel like they haven't yet cemented their legacy or achieved a specific athletic goal, that could make the possibility of retirement even more bittersweet. Professional careers are on average short note (3.5 to 6 years in the Big Four North American leagues — Major League Baseball , National Basketball Association , National Football League , and National Hockey League . The crème de la crème might be able to play for longer, but will likely still retire by their early 40s. This relatively early retirement age may trigger a crisis about figuring out what to do with the rest of their lives after dedicating decades to their sport. Some athletes respond to this more gracefully than others. They might try to do one final season and achieve something great. Some might live in denial and resort to less-than-savory methods to try and maintain their status, such as cheating or substance abuse (the usual suspects are stimulants, pain medication, steroids , or a combination thereof). Others proactively move to another role in the sports world (e.g., becoming a commentator of some sort or a mentor for younger athletes — indeed, many coaches are also former pros). They might even become Old Masters despite the aches and pains . Some may attempt a comeback after years of retirement; outcomes vary. Others content themselves with their memories of the Glory Days . Contrast Jaded Washout and I Coulda Been a Contender! , who probably never made it far to begin with. See also White-Dwarf Starlet , who's still holding on to their long-gone fame, and Hollywood Old . Age Insecurity is a supertrope for charactera angsting about growing old. This is more of a characterization trope than one that deals in exact ages. Specific sports vary in their overall turnover rates and what years are considered the usual peak because specific abilities being called on can vary — a 27-year-old figure skater might have similar aging pains as a 37-year-old tennis player, for example. See Ring Oldies for older professional wrestlers.
